claims.text: Vic Tandy diagnosed 'haunted' sensations at Warwick Labs as infrasound near 19 Hz produced by a building fan that resonated with the human eyeball.

evidence.quote:
vic tandy was
working at warwick labs

calculated the
frequency of the sound at about 19 hertz

claims.rationale: The Tandy / Coventry infrasound ghost story is published in Journal of the Society for Psychical Research (1998) and Tandy's 2000 follow-up. The 19 Hz fan and the 'sense of presence' are documented. Eyeball-resonance is a partial explanation often debated but with experimental support.

claims.text: The range of human hearing begins at 20 Hz, so anything below (infrasound) is inaudible but can still physically affect the body.

evidence.quote:
fan wasn't audible it's what's
known as infrasound

claims.rationale: Standard acoustic definition: 20 Hz-20 kHz is the nominal human audibility range; <20 Hz = infrasound. Infrasound's physiological effects at sufficient SPL are documented.

claims.text: A tiger's roar contains 18 Hz components that disorient prey long enough for an attack.

evidence.quote:
tiger attacks its roar contains
frequencies at 18 hertz

claims.rationale: Tiger and lion roars do contain low-frequency components down to ~18 Hz (von Muggenthaler's Fauna Communications research). The 'disorient' claim is the common interpretation but not rigorously proven as causative in hunts.

claims.text: The 'brown note' — a 5–9 Hz frequency that makes humans lose bowel control — has not actually been proven to work.

evidence.quote:
brown note is said to occur at
frequencies between five and nine hertz

nobody's been able
to actually prove it yet

claims.rationale: MythBusters S4 E8 (2005) tested and did not reproduce the brown note effect. No peer-reviewed demonstration exists. The 'hasn't been proven' framing matches the actual state of evidence. Host is effectively denying the popular myth.

claims.text: The US space program conducted tests in which brown-note frequencies were transferred directly to subjects' bodies through vibrating cockpit chairs.

evidence.quote:
united states space program
conducted tests

claims.rationale: NASA and USAF did perform whole-body vibration studies in the 1960s (e.g. NASA TN D-3267) that documented physiological responses to low-frequency mechanical vibration via pilot seats. The 'brown note' framing is popular-press; the underlying research is real.

dependencies.rationale: [alternative] Provides an alternative mechanism (direct body coupling) under which sub-audible frequencies demonstrably affect humans, even though the airborne brown-note myth failed.

claims.text: Seven hertz is especially dangerous because it matches the median alpha rhythm of the brain and the resonant frequency of many body organs.

evidence.quote:
most dangerous frequencies to humans
are at about seven hertz this is the
median alpha rhythm of the brain

claims.rationale: Alpha rhythm is ~8-13 Hz (not strictly 'median 7 Hz') and organ resonance is spread across a range. Claim oversimplifies. Some truth in that abdominal organs do resonate around 4-8 Hz, but the 'especially dangerous' framing is overstated in the literature.

claims.text: Russian-born French scientist Vladimir Gavreau invented infrasonic weapons in 1957 after being asked to cure an unknown illness at a Marseille plant.

evidence.quote:
gavrow became interested in sound
research in 1957

claims.rationale: Vladimir Gavreau did work on infrasound weapons at the Laboratoire d'Electroacoustique Marseille and publish on the topic in the 1960s. The 1957 Marseille-plant origin story is widely repeated but the illness-diagnosis narrative mixes anecdote and fact. Weapon-prototype claims are partially supported.

claims.text: Long exposure to ~7 Hz infrasound at high enough amplitude causes panic, convulsions, vomiting and eventually organ rupture and death.

evidence.quote:
panic
convulsions vomiting and with long
enough exposure organ rupture and death

claims.rationale: [alternative] The lethal-infrasound claim originates from Gavreau's self-reported tests but has not been independently replicated. Modern reviews (e.g. Leventhall 2006) conclude infrasound at believable urban SPLs does not produce the catastrophic physiological effects described. Organ rupture from 7 Hz infrasound is not demonstrated.

dependencies.rationale: Describes the damaging effects claimed by Gavreau's weapon program.

claims.text: The US Navy uses ultrasound weapons to repel pirates.

evidence.quote:
ultrasound is such an
effective weapon that has been used by
the us navy to repel pirates

claims.rationale: LRAD (Long Range Acoustic Device) is well-documented in anti-piracy deployments — notably the 2005 Seabourn Spirit incident off Somalia. Technically LRAD is high-intensity audible sound rather than strict ultrasound, but the substantive claim about sonic anti-pirate use is accurate.

claims.text: 'Havana Syndrome' — unexplained health incidents affecting US intelligence officers and diplomats in Cuba since 2016 — is likely caused by a sonic or directed pulsed radio-frequency weapon.

evidence.quote:
american intelligence officers and
diplomats in cuba and it's now known as
havana syndrome

directed pulsed radio
frequency energy could be responsible

claims.rationale: The existence of Havana Syndrome is real; the causation is contested. The 2020 National Academies report flagged pulsed RF as one hypothesis, but the 2023 ODNI intelligence community assessment concluded 'very unlikely' that a foreign adversary caused the symptoms. Most cases now attributed to environmental factors / preexisting conditions / stress. Sonic-weapon etiology has lost support.

claims.text: Russia is the likely source of Havana Syndrome attacks according to intelligence officers, but Russia denies any involvement and no evidence has been publicly disclosed.

evidence.quote:
intelligence officers are quietly
pointing the finger at russia

russians have to say about it oh
they deny any involvement

claims.rationale: [undercuts] The 2023 IC assessment explicitly contradicted the Russia-did-it framing, concluding it was 'very unlikely' a foreign adversary was responsible. The intelligence officers 'pointing the finger' pattern was a real 2021-era view but has since been walked back officially.

dependencies.rationale: Extends the Havana Syndrome claim with a specific (and disputed) attribution.

claims.text: Two White House staff members experienced Havana-Syndrome-like symptoms while working at the White House.

evidence.quote:
last year two
white house staff experienced symptoms

claims.rationale: White House / Ellipse Havana-Syndrome incidents in November 2020 and 2021 were widely reported and officially acknowledged as Anomalous Health Incidents by the US government. The event did happen; the sonic-weapon causation is separate.

dependencies.rationale: Expanded geography of Havana Syndrome cases supports the existence claim.
